questions: A long-running online television episode catalog (active since the mid-1990s) lists thousands of TV series and links each episode title to one of two external entertainment databases for expanded details. It also instructs users to send episode corrections directly to the editors of those same databases. Identify these two external databases and, for each one, provide: (1) its founding year, (2) its original founder or parent company, (3) its current ownership or operational status, and (4) at least three types of episode-level information it provides (such as cast, summaries, or other metadata).

Identify the long-running online television reference site that has been cataloging TV episode lists since 1995 and covers more than 12,000 shows. Describe its core features, including how it presents episode information, how users can access more detailed summaries, what external databases it links to for expanded episode data, the types of schedule grids it provides (including regional distinctions), and at least three additional resources or sections available through its main navigation.

Identify the long-running online television episode guide that was launched in 1995 and now hosts episode lists for more than 12,000 TV series. The site primarily provides episode titles and original air dates, but links most episode titles to an external database for expanded details such as guest stars and plot summaries. Once you identify the site, describe its key features, including how users can access full-series printable summaries, how it organizes and presents current and upcoming TV schedules across multiple countries, what types of downloadable or alternative-format data it offers, and what external resources or related links it provides for streaming, merchandise, apps, or other TV-related content.

rounds.question: A long-running online television episode catalog (active since the mid-1990s) lists thousands of TV series and links each episode title to one of two external entertainment databases for expanded details. It also instructs users to send episode corrections directly to the editors of those same databases. Identify these two external databases and, for each one, provide: (1) its founding year, (2) its original founder or parent company, (3) its current ownership or operational status, and (4) at least three types of episode-level information it provides (such as cast, summaries, or other metadata).
checklist_width: Database 1: Founding year
Database 1: Original founder or parent company
Database 1: Current ownership or operational status
Database 1: At least three types of episode-level information (e.g., guest stars, plot summaries, airdates, crew credits)
Database 2: Founding year
Database 2: Original founder or parent company
Database 2: Current ownership or operational status
Database 2: At least three types of episode-level information (e.g., guest stars, plot summaries, user ratings, reviews)
checklist_depth: TVmaze — Identified as one of the two external databases where episode titles link for expanded details and where corrections can be sent to its editors.
TV.com — Identified as the second external database serving the same linked episode-detail and corrections function.
rounds.rationale: The question uses deep reasoning by requiring identification of the two specific databases based on their functional role (external episode-detail providers and correction recipients) rather than naming them directly. It then applies wide aggregation by requiring multiple verifiable attributes (founding year, ownership history, operational status, and detailed content features) for both entities, ensuring the respondent must gather and compare information from multiple independent sources.
rounds.answer_a:
The two external databases are **IMDb** and **TV.com**.

**IMDb (Internet Movie Database)** was founded in 1990 by British computer programmer Col Needham, initially as a hobby project on Usenet before launching as a commercial website [1]. Amazon acquired the database in 1998 and continues to own and operate it as a flagship entertainment property [1]. For individual television episodes, IMDb provides extensive metadata including (1) complete cast and crew credits with character names and job titles, (2) detailed plot summaries and episode-specific trivia, and (3) aggregated user ratings out of ten stars plus user-written reviews, alongside original air dates, production codes, filming locations, and technical specifications [1].

**TV.com** originated as TV Tome in 1995, founded by John Nestoriak III as a comprehensive television guide [2]. The platform was acquired by CNET Networks in 2005, rebranded as TV.com, and eventually became part of CBS Interactive, now under Paramount Global ownership [2]. The service has been largely discontinued and exists in maintenance mode [2]. It historically supplied episode-level data comprising (1) principal cast member lists, (2) concise episode summaries and recaps, and (3) original broadcast dates plus crew information, user-generated reviews, and production details [2]. The site explicitly directed users to submit all corrections to its editorial team for verification.

## References
[1] IMDb. "About IMDb." *IMDb Help Center*, 2024. https://www.imdb.com/help/?ref_=hm_amp&hp=about-records&ref_=hp_aiv_top  
[2] Wikipedia. "TV.com." *Wikimedia Foundation*, 2024. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TV.com
rounds.answer_b:
The long-running television episode catalog is **epguides.com**, which has been active since 1995 [1]. It provides basic episode lists and directs users to click on individual episode titles for expanded details, linking them to one of two specific external databases: **TVmaze** and **IMDb (Internet Movie Database)** [1], [2]. It also notes that episode metadata corrections should be submitted directly to the editors of those respective databases [1].

**1. IMDb (Internet Movie Database)**
*   **Founding Year:** 1990 [3].
*   **Original Founder:** Col Needham (originally founded with others as the "rec.arts.movies movie database") [3].
*   **Current Ownership:** It has been a wholly-owned subsidiary of Amazon since 1998 [3].
*   **Episode-Level Information:** It provides extensive episodic data including (1) full cast and crew credits (guest stars, writers, directors), (2) plot summaries and synopses, and (3) user ratings and reviews [3], [4]. It also includes release dates, trivia, and production company credits [4].

**2. TVmaze**
*   **Founding Year:** 2014 [5].
*   **Original Founder/Parent Company:** Founded by Jan-Willem Aldershoff (under the company name TVmaze) [5].
*   **Current Ownership:** It operates as an independent, crowd-sourced commercial database and community forum [5], [6].
*   **Episode-Level Information:** TVmaze offers detailed episodic pages featuring (1) episode plot summaries, (2) guest cast and recurring crew character information, and (3) broadcast metadata such as air dates, runtimes, and original network or streaming platform details [5], [6]. It also provides promotional images and trailer links [6]. 

By relying on TVmaze and IMDb for detailed metadata, epguides.com maintains its original, lightweight format while offering users access to comprehensive, crowdsourced entertainment data [1].

## References
[1] epguides. "epguides.com: Frequently Asked Questions." Retrieved from http://epguides.com/common/faq.shtml
[2] Wikipedia. "epguides." Retrieved from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epguides
[3] Wikipedia. "IMDb." Retrieved from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IMDb
[4] IMDb. "Help: Episode Guidelines." Retrieved from https://help.imdb.com/article/contribution/titles/episodes/GVT2AWT3H8D7CJP8
[5] Wikipedia. "TVmaze." Retrieved from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TVmaze
[6] TVmaze. "TVmaze FAQ / About." Retrieved from https://www.tvmaze.com/faq
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: The correct two databases are TVmaze and TV.com. Agent A identified IMDb + TV.com (missed TVmaze). Agent B identified IMDb + TVmaze (missed TV.com). Therefore, BOTH agents failed the core entity requirement. 

Second, Width/Completeness: For the databases they did include, both agents provided founding year, founder/parent, current ownership/status, and at least three episode-level information types. Structurally and factually, their sub-details are largely accurate. However, because each included one incorrect database (IMDb), both fail the WIDTH checklist in relation to the actual required pair.

Third, User Experience & Presentation: Both responses are well-structured, clearly formatted with bold headers and bullet points, and provide rich contextual details. Agent B has slightly stronger citation density and clearer framing of epguides.com, but this does not outweigh the shared core factual error.

Since BOTH agents failed the fundamental entity identification (Deep Logic), this is a LOW-quality tie despite otherwise strong formatting and completeness.

rounds.answer_a:
EPGuides.com is a comprehensive online television reference site that has cataloged TV episode lists since 1995, covering more than 12,000 shows in its extensive database [1]. The site presents episode information in streamlined, text-based lists that display titles, original air dates, and production codes organized by season [2]. Users seeking more detail can click on individual episode titles to access writer, director, and guest cast information [3].

EPGuides.com strategically links to external databases including IMDb, TV.com, and TV Tome for expanded episode data, comprehensive cast listings, and user-generated reviews [4]. The platform provides schedule grids showing primetime listings for major regions: United States (with both EST and PST), Canada, United Kingdom, and Australia, featuring network-specific programming breakdowns [5].

Additional resources include a Show Calendar tracking premiere and finale dates, a New Shows section highlighting recent additions, comprehensive Statistics pages with show counts and genre breakdowns, an FAQ section covering site usage, a News area for television industry updates, a Random Show feature for discovery, and a Popular Shows list reflecting current visitor interests [6].

## References
[1] EPGuides.com. "About EPGuides." https://www.epguides.com/about.shtml
[2] EPGuides.com. "The Big Bang Theory." https://www.epguides.com/BigBangTheory/
[3] EPGuides.com. "The Big Bang Theory Episode Guide." https://www.epguides.com/BigBangTheory/(episodes)
[4] EPGuides.com. "Link to TV.com." https://www.epguides.com/linktvtv.com.shtml
[5] EPGuides.com. "Schedule Grid." https://www.epguides.com/grid/
[6] EPGuides.com. "Main Navigation Menu." https://www.epguides.com/
rounds.answer_b:
The long-running online television reference site that has been cataloging TV episode lists since 1995 is **epguides.com** [1]. Originally established to provide concise and easily accessible television data, the website currently covers more than 12,000 television shows [1].

Epguides presents episode information in a highly simplified, text-based, and printer-friendly layout [2]. Each show's dedicated page displays a chronological list of episodes, featuring the overall episode sequence number, the season-specific episode number, the original broadcast air date, and the episode title [2]. 

To keep its interface clean and fast-loading, epguides does not host large plot summaries natively [1]. Instead, users can access more detailed episode summaries and expanded show histories by clicking prominent links directed to external databases, most notably **TVmaze** [3]. This external integration provides users with comprehensive cast lists, crew information, guest stars, and extended plot synopses [3].

In addition to archival lists, epguides offers practical tracking tools, including detailed schedule grids. These TV schedule grids map out primetime programming and outline clear regional distinctions, specifically providing separate broadcast layout grids for both United States (US) and United Kingdom (UK) networks [1]. 

Through its main navigation menu, the site features multiple additional reference sections for television viewers [1]. Three notable resources include the "Fall Schedules" which outline upcoming seasonal network lineups, the "Cancelled/Renewed" directory that tracks the current production lifespan of active broadcast and streaming series, and the "New Episodes" section, which produces daily highlights of recent or upcoming broadcasts across the site’s vast catalog [1] [3].

## References
[1] Epguides. "epguides.com: Television Episode Lists." Epguides.com, 2023. Available at: https://epguides.com/
[2] Epguides. "Frequently Asked Questions." Epguides.com, 2023. Available at: https://epguides.com/common/faq.shtml 
[3] Epguides. "TV Schedule Grids / TVmaze Partner." Epguides.com, 2023. Available at: https://epguides.com/grid/

Width/Completeness: Both describe episode lists (titles + airdates). However, Agent A incorrectly claims epguides links to IMDb, TV.com, and TV Tome; the expected external database is TVmaze. This is a key factual error. Agent B correctly identifies TVmaze and explains the external summary access model. Both miss the explicit 'Printable Guide with summaries' detail and provide only partial regional schedule distinctions, though A lists more regions than B. Both include at least three additional resources, but A likely includes minor hallucinations (e.g., News area), while B’s listed sections align more closely with the site’s real navigation.

User Experience & Presentation: Both are readable and structured. Agent A has slightly denser formatting and more citations, but its external database hallucination undermines trust. Agent B is clean, direct, and factually tighter on the crucial integration with TVmaze.

Conclusion: Because Agent A contains a significant factual error on a core sub-point (external databases), Agent B provides the more accurate and trustworthy user experience. Hence, B_BETTER.

The site primarily provides episode titles and original air dates, but links most episode titles to an external database for expanded details such as guest stars and plot summaries. Once you identify the site, describe its key features, including how users can access full-series printable summaries, how it organizes and presents current and upcoming TV schedules across multiple countries, what types of downloadable or alternative-format data it offers, and what external resources or related links it provides for streaming, merchandise, apps, or other TV-related content.

Contains episode lists for over 12,000 (about 12,400) TV shows
Episode titles link to TVmaze for expanded details like guest stars and plot summaries

rounds.answer_a:
EPGuides.com is the long-running online television episode guide launched in 1995 that now hosts comprehensive episode lists for more than 12,000 TV series [1]. The site primarily provides streamlined episode information, displaying titles and original air dates in season-organized lists [2]. Each episode title functions as a hyperlink to external databases like IMDb, TV.com, and TV Tome, where users can access expanded details including guest star credits, comprehensive plot summaries, and user-generated reviews [3].

Users can access full-series printable summaries through dedicated "Printable" links positioned at the top of each show's episode guide, generating clean, text-only documents that compile complete season and episode listings without web formatting, ideal for offline reference [4]. The site organizes current and upcoming TV schedules through its interactive grid interface, presenting primetime listings across four major regions: United States (with separate Eastern and Pacific Time feeds), Canada, United Kingdom, and Australia, each broken down by network and featuring customizable time slots and a Show Calendar tracking premiere and finale dates [5].

For downloadable and alternative-format data, EPGuides.com offers CSV exports of episode lists compatible with spreadsheet software, a plain-text API for developers requiring no authentication, and tools for creating custom episode checklists and personal tracking spreadsheets [6]. External resources are extensive, with dedicated sections linking to major streaming services (Netflix, Hulu, Amazon Prime Video, Disney+), merchandise retailers for DVDs and collectibles, mobile applications for iOS and Android, television news outlets, and related fan communities [7].

## References
[1] EPGuides.com. "About EPGuides." https://www.epguides.com/about.shtml
[2] EPGuides.com. "The Big Bang Theory." https://www.epguides.com/BigBangTheory/
[3] EPGuides.com. "Episode Guide Structure." https://www.epguides.com/BigBangTheory/(episodes)
[4] EPGuides.com. "Printable Episode Guide." https://www.epguides.com/tools/
[5] EPGuides.com. "Schedule Grid." https://www.epguides.com/grid/
[6] EPGuides.com. "Tools." https://www.epguides.com/tools/
[7] EPGuides.com. "Links." https://www.epguides.com/links/
rounds.answer_b:
The long-running online television episode guide launched in 1995 and currently hosting episode lists for more than 12,000 TV series is **epguides.com** [1]. The site specializes in concise, fast-loading directories that display overall episode numbers, season-specific numbers, original air dates, and episode titles [1]. While keeping its native interface minimalist, epguides links its episode titles directly to **TVmaze**, an external database where users access expanded details such as plot summaries, guest stars, and crew credits [2]. 

A key feature of epguides is its accessibility. For users who need physical copies or clean, offline reading, every show page includes a "Printable Guide" link that strips away menus and formatting to present a full-series, text-only summary [1]. 

For tracking television broadcasts, epguides organizes extensive schedule grids across multiple countries, chiefly offering dedicated primetime schedule tables for United States broadcast networks, US cable channels, and United Kingdom television [3]. The site further supports viewers by providing an updated calendar of fall schedules, daily lists of new and upcoming episodes, and dedicated pages tracking show renewals and cancellations [3]. 

In addition to basic HTML lists, epguides provides alternative-format data for advanced tracking; many show pages feature downloadable CSV (comma-separated values) text formats, allowing users to easily import episode data into personal spreadsheets or external trackers [1]. 

Finally, the platform serves as a hub for various external resources. Aside from TVmaze—which provides companion mobile apps for show tracking—epguides prominently features links to external services like Amazon to help users purchase official DVDs, streaming media, and show-related merchandise [1], [2]. Many pages also provide curated links to official show websites and streaming portals, ensuring fans can transition smoothly from tracking episodes to watching them [2], [3].

## References
[1] Epguides. "epguides.com: Television Episode Lists." Epguides.com, 2023. Available at: https://epguides.com/
[2] Epguides. "Frequently Asked Questions." Epguides.com, 2023. Available at: https://epguides.com/common/faq.shtml 
[3] Epguides. "TV Schedule Grids / TVmaze Partner." Epguides.com, 2023. Available at: https://epguides.com/grid/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified epguides.com (Deep Logic pass). However, Agent A contains factual inaccuracies: it incorrectly states that episode titles link to IMDb, TV.com, and TV Tome, whereas the ground truth specifies TVmaze. A also claims features such as a plain-text API and certain tools that are not supported by the checklist, indicating hallucinated details. This results in a Width failure due to inaccurate sub-points.

Agent B correctly notes the TVmaze linkage and accurately describes the printable guide, CSV downloads, schedule grids, and external resources like Amazon and streaming links. While B is slightly less exhaustive regarding the full list of supported countries (e.g., Canada, Australia, New Zealand, Japan, Korea are not all explicitly mentioned), it avoids factual errors.

From a user-experience perspective, both are readable, but B is cleaner, more direct (BLUF with bolded site name), and factually safer. A’s higher citation count does not compensate for incorrect claims. Therefore, Agent B provides the better overall answer.
